source: arxiv: Forecasting NCAA Basketball Outcomes with LSTM and Transformer Models (2025); MDPI Uncertainty-Aware NBA Forecasting (Jan 2026)
title: Cat50: Temporal Sequence Encoding — Last-N-Game Ordered Features
rationale: Current rolling averages (Cat1, Cat17) collapse temporal order — a team that went W-W-W-L-L has the same 3-game average as one that went L-L-L-W-W, despite opposite momentum. LSTM/Transformer models show strong improvement by preserving sequence order. For CPU-only XGBoost we can extract the sequence structure as explicit ordered features without neural networks, getting most of the benefit.

paper_1.title: Forecasting NCAA Basketball Outcomes with Deep Learning: LSTM and Transformer Models
paper_1.key_finding: Sequential encoding of game outcomes outperforms static rolling features; LSTM captures momentum breaks that rolling averages miss

paper_2.title: Uncertainty-Aware Machine Learning for NBA Forecasting (MDPI Jan 2026)
paper_2.key_finding: Rolling-form indicators + recurrent input captures short-term momentum and roster shocks not reflected in market prices. Brier 0.199 achieved.

feature_groups.name: Last-10 margin sequence (home team)
feature_groups.encoding: point differential, normalized by season std

feature_groups.name: Last-10 opponent strength sequence (home team)
feature_groups.encoding: opponent ELO at time of game, normalized

implementation.nan_handling: fill with team season average if < 10 games available

prerequisites: team_games_lookup dict must be built before feature generation (similar to existing ELO lookup)
games must be sorted chronologically within team
Cat50 should run after Cat6 (REST & SCHEDULE) which builds the game history structure
risks: Data leakage: ensure all lookups use strict < game_date (not <=)
Memory: storing last-10-games per team per date adds ~80 columns to feature matrix
Compute: 10 lookups per team × 2 teams × 9551 games = ~191k lookups — acceptable on CPU

cross_project.political_version: Encode last-N political events (votes, executive orders, FEC filings) as ordered sequence features — same principle, different domain
